Mercedes B‑Class B200 CDI / B200d Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes B‑Class B200 CDI / B200d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned B200 CDI engine (OM651.901) typically costs £2,793–£3,436 supply only. A reconditioned B200d engine (OM654q) typically costs £2,800–£3,800 supply only. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes B‑Class B200 CDI / B200d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 (W246) is a durable engine with a typical lifespan of 200,000–250,000 miles with proper maintenance. However, it has a well‑documented weakness: timing chain stretch. The OM654q (W247) is a newer design with improved power, but a cylinder head bolt recall has been issued. Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes B‑Class B200 CDI / B200d?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the W246 (OM651), the most common issue is timing chain stretch – causing rattling on cold start. Other issues include 7G‑DCT transmission problems (jerky shifts, shuddering), DPF blockage, and turbocharger failure. On the W247 (OM654q), a cylinder head bolt recall has been issued.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a B200 CDI / B200d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £4,000–£10,000 B‑Class, a £2,793–£3,800 recon is 30‑60% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the timing chain has snapped on an OM651, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my B‑Class's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W246 (2012–2018) B200 CDI uses the OM651.901 – a 2.1‑litre four‑cylinder turbodiesel. The W247 (2018–present) B200d uses the OM654q – a 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder turbodiesel. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a B200 CDI / B200d engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – OM651 timing chain wear (if caught early), 7G‑DCT transmission issues, DPF blockage, turbocharger failure – are repairable. OM654q cylinder head bolt issues are covered by recall. However, if the timing chain has snapped on an OM651, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes B‑Class B200 CDI / B200d Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: OM651 no longer available new; OM654q available from Mercedes

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the B200 CDI/B200d pricing. A replacement OM651.901 engine costs on average £2,793 in good condition, with new or reconditioned units costing up to £3,436. Reconditioned engines typically range from £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. As a front‑wheel‑drive vehicle with a transverse engine, labour costs are slightly higher than for rear‑wheel‑drive models.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a B‑Class CDI engine typically takes 10‑14 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist). The transverse engine mounting and front‑wheel‑drive layout add complexity.

cta.label: → Compare B‑Class B200 CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: OM651 Timing Chain Stretch (W246, 2012–2018)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or clattering noise from the engine for 2‑5 seconds after cold start, metallic ticking, engine warning light, rough running, loss of power. The timing chain is at the rear of the engine, making access labour‑intensive.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–100,000 miles — many owners report noise around 90,000 miles.
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£2,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (timing chain, tensioner, and guide replacement — engine or gearbox removal required).
problems.replacementCost: £2,793–£3,800 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The OM651 timing chain is the defining weakness of the W246 B200 CDI. The chain is mounted at the rear of the engine, making replacement a very big job that likely requires engine or gearbox removal. The stop‑start system causes additional stress to the chain. A £1,200–£2,500 preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a £2,793–£3,800 engine replacement. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing system inspected immediately. Use a genuine Mercedes tensioner — not aftermarket.

problems.title: OM654q Cylinder Head Bolt Failure (W247, 2018–present)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, oil loss, overheating, engine warning light, rough running. A cylinder head bolt might break, causing the connection between cylinder head and crankcase to partially loosen.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — recall affects certain A‑Class, B‑Class and GLA vehicles with OM654q engines.
problems.repairCost: £0 (if recalled — dealer repair free) / £500–£1,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (if out of warranty).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored coolant/oil loss can cause engine damage).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice coolant or oil loss, or engine warning lights, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: A recall has been issued for OM654q engines where a cylinder head bolt might break. If this occurs, the connection between the cylinder head and crankcase might partially loosen, potentially leading to a loss of engine coolant and/or slight amounts of engine oil. Check your VIN against the recall database immediately. If you notice any coolant or oil loss, have the vehicle inspected promptly.

problems.title: 7G‑DCT Transmission Issues (W246, 2012–2018)
problems.symptoms: Jerky gear changes, particularly at lower speeds or in stop‑and‑go traffic, delayed engagement when shifting from park to drive or reverse, transmission overheating, shuddering on start‑up.
problems.repairCost: £500–£2,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (TCM software update, clutch replacement, or transmission overhaul).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if jerky shifts or shuddering occurs.

problems.recommendation: The 7‑speed dual‑clutch transmission (7G‑DCT) in the W246 B200 CDI has known issues. Jerky gear changes, particularly at low speeds, and delayed engagement are common complaints. A transmission control module (TCM) software update may resolve the issue; in more severe cases, clutch replacement may be required. One owner reported a shudder on start‑up with an estimated repair cost of Rs. 3 Lakhs for flywheel replacement. Regular transmission fluid changes are essential.

problems.title: DPF Blockage & Turbocharger Issues (W246 & W247)
problems.symptoms: Reduced power, increased fuel consumption, engine warning light, DPF warning message, black smoke on acceleration, whining noise from the engine bay, turbo lag.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (DPF cleaning, forced regeneration, or turbocharger repair/replacement).

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if DPF warning appears or turbo noise develops.

problems.recommendation: The B200 CDI's DPF can become clogged, especially if the car is frequently used for short trips where the exhaust system doesn't reach optimal temperature for regeneration. Symptoms include reduced power, increased fuel consumption, and warning lights. Turbocharger problems — such as turbo lag, reduced power, or a whining noise — are often caused by carbon buildup or worn internal components. Regular motorway runs (30+ minutes at 60+ mph) are essential to maintain the DPF. Using high‑quality diesel and regular servicing can prevent turbo issues.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes B‑Class B200 CDI / B200d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Full engine replacement — valve damage is likely
Recall repair is free; out‑of‑warranty repair still cheaper than engine replacement
Worth fixing — TCM update or clutch replacement
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes B‑Class B200 CDI / B200d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
* W246 (OM651): Timing chain quiet — no rattling on cold start
* W247 (OM654q): Recall work verified as completed — cylinder head bolt issue is critical
* W246: 7G‑DCT transmission serviced — fluid changes are essential
* A well‑maintained example — the OM651 is durable with proper care
avoidIf: * W246: Any sign of timing chain rattle on cold start — this is the defining weakness
* W247: No evidence of recall completion — cylinder head bolt failure is a safety issue
* W246: Any sign of jerky gear changes — 7G‑DCT issues can be expensive
* Poor service history — these engines are intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes B‑Class B200 CDI and B200d are efficient and refined diesel‑powered MPVs. The W246 (2012–2018) with the OM651 engine is a durable powerplant with a well‑documented Achilles heel: rear‑mounted timing chain stretch. The W247 (2018–present) with the OM654q engine is a newer design with improved power and efficiency, but a cylinder head bolt recall has been issued. Buy on service history above all else — a vehicle with documented timing chain maintenance (W246) or recall completion (W247) is a safe bet; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare B‑Class B200 CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes B‑Class B200 CDI / B200d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any rattling for 2‑5 seconds? (OM651 timing chain warning — stop immediately)
2. Service History — Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
3. Timing Chain History — W246 (OM651): Has the chain been inspected or replaced?
4. Recall Status — W247 (OM654q): Cylinder head bolt recall completed? This is critical
5. Transmission — W246: Any jerky shifts, shuddering, or delayed engagement? (7G‑DCT warning)
6. DPF Health — Has the vehicle done regular longer runs? (short trips kill DPFs)
7. Turbo Health — Any whining noise, turbo lag, or smoke? (turbo warning)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
